children.value: “We’ve been simulating everything. When can we use formulas instead?”

All previous chapters built understanding through simulation — we shuffled,
resampled, and generated. But many textbooks give you formulas directly.
When are those formulas valid? And when does simulation win?
Many analytic results only hold when data is normally distributed.

children.html: X∼N(μ,σ2)X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)X∼N(μ,σ2)
Y∼N(ν,τ2)Y \sim N(\nu, \tau^2)Y∼N(ν,τ2)
X+Y∼N(μ+ν,σ2+τ2)X + Y \sim N(\mu + \nu, \sigma^2 + \tau^2)X+Y∼N(μ+ν,σ2+τ2)

children.value: Sums of normals are normal. This is special — it doesn’t hold for most distributions.

Xˉ1−Xˉ2∼N ⁣(μ1−μ2,σ12n1+σ22n2)\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2 \sim N\!\left(\mu_1 - \mu_2, \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}\right)Xˉ1​−Xˉ2​∼N(μ1​−μ2​,n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​)

children.value:
This is how the classical two-sample t-test is derived — it assumes both populations
are normal and uses the above formula for the sampling distribution.
The most important theorem in statistics.
For any population with finite mean

children.value:
It is the reason normal-based formulas work in so many
situations even when the data is not normal — we’re taking means of large samples,
and means are approximately normal.
